message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3467231/
**Title:** Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation (PMC)
**Found via:** perceptual limits of accelerated speech search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: Extract key findings about perceptual learning of time-compressed speech. Specifically: what compression rates/ratios were tested, what are the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, how training/adaptation improves comprehension of fast speech, the time scales of adaptation, what signal-processing/compression methods were used, and any quantitative results on intelligibility at different speeds. Provide direct verbatim quotes for each finding. Also note the publication date and authors/journal.

content.content:
# Key Findings on Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech

**Publication:** PLoS One, October 9, 2012
**Authors:** Karen Banai & Yizhar Lavner

## Compression Rates Tested

Initial compression started at 65% of original duration, with a range down to 20% maximum compression. The researchers note: "Listeners were presented with sentences compressed to a predetermined fraction (30–45%) of their original duration."

## Perceptual Limits & Initial Performance

Performance without training was severely limited initially: "Initially, listeners were able to reconstruct only part (20–76%) of the words in any given sentence."

Even with brief exposure: "Brief adaptation (10–20 sentences) significantly improved identification, but left performance well below perfect (40–85% correct)."

## Training-Induced Improvement

After multiday training, non-native speakers achieved native-level performance: "Performance of the trained group was equivalent to that of the native speakers on all conditions on which learning occurred."

Quantitative gains over 10-14 days showed: "Mean verification thresholds improved by 6±10% on average" between first and last training sessions.

## Time Scales of Adaptation

Two distinct phases emerged:
- **Rapid phase:** "Identification of time-compressed sentences improves rapidly with repeated exposure" (10-20 sentences)
- **Extended phase:** "Multiday training on time-compressed speech results in significantly more perceptual learning than brief adaptation."

## Signal Processing Method

"Time compression throughout the experiment was carried out using an implementation of the WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap and Add) algorithm."

content.text: I have the key findings. Let me verify a couple of the specific quotes since the WebFetch summary mixed some numbers. Let me fetch the methods section detail to confirm the compression ratios and WSOLA claim verbatim.

input.prompt: Quote verbatim any sentences mentioning: (1) the WSOLA algorithm and how time compression was performed; (2) the exact compression rates/percentages of original duration tested; (3) the difference in learning between brief adaptation and multiday training; (4) any statements about the perceptual limit or rate at which time-compressed speech becomes unintelligible. Give exact verbatim sentences only.

content.content:
# Key Findings on Time-Compressed Speech Research

## (1) WSOLA Algorithm and Time Compression Method

"Time compression throughout the experiment was carried out using an implementation of the WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap and Add) algorithm, which has been shown to achieve very high quality time-scale modification of speech signals."

## (2) Compression Rates Tested

"A two-down one-up procedure was employed, with an initial compression level of 65%. Compression level was modified logarithmically, using a scale of 25 logarithmically equal steps between the starting value and the maximal level of compression (20%)."

Additionally: "Listeners were presented with sentences compressed to a predetermined fraction (30–45%) of their original duration."

## (3) Difference Between Brief Adaptation and Multiday Training

"Multiday training on time-compressed speech results in significantly more perceptual learning than brief adaptation."

Further detail: "Trained listeners learned significantly more than controls on the trained condition" with multiday practice showing greater gains than the control group.

## (4) Perceptual Limit or Unintelligibility Rate

The document does not contain explicit statements about a perceptual limit or rate at which time-compressed speech becomes unintelligible.

content.text: I have sufficient verified information to extract the claims.

claims.claim: Time compression of speech in this study was performed using the WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap and Add) algorithm, which preserves pitch while shortening duration and achieves high-quality time-scale modification of speech.
claims.quote: Time compression throughout the experiment was carried out using an implementation of the WSOLA (Waveform Similarity Overlap and Add) algorithm, which has been shown to achieve very high quality time-scale modification of speech signals.

claims.claim: Comprehension of time-compressed speech improves rapidly with brief exposure (10-20 sentences), but this rapid adaptation leaves performance well below ceiling, indicating brief adaptation alone is insufficient for full intelligibility at high compression.
claims.quote: Brief adaptation (10–20 sentences) significantly improved identification, but left performance well below perfect (40–85% correct).

claims.claim: Extended multiday training produces substantially more perceptual learning of time-compressed speech than brief single-session adaptation, showing that adaptation to accelerated speech has both a rapid phase and a slower training-dependent phase.
claims.quote: Multiday training on time-compressed speech results in significantly more perceptual learning than brief adaptation.

claims.claim: Speech was compressed down to as little as 20% of its original duration (equivalent to roughly 5x speed-up), with sentences typically tested at 30-45% of original duration, and listeners could be trained to comprehend these heavily compressed signals.
claims.quote: Compression level was modified logarithmically, using a scale of 25 logarithmically equal steps between the starting value and the maximal level of compression (20%).

claims.claim: With multiday training, non-native listeners reached comprehension performance equivalent to native speakers on trained conditions, demonstrating that training can close large initial intelligibility gaps for accelerated speech.
claims.quote: Performance of the trained group was equivalent to that of the native speakers on all conditions on which learning occurred.

content.text:
Extracted claims from the PMC article (Banai & Lavner, PLoS One, 2012-10-09), a primary peer-reviewed research source.

Key findings relevant to the research question:
- The study used WSOLA for high-quality time-scale modification (directly relevant to the TSM portion of the question).
- It quantifies the perceptual-limits/adaptation angle: brief adaptation (10-20 sentences) improves but caps comprehension well below perfect, while multiday training yields substantially more learning.
- Compression went as deep as 20% of original duration (~5x), with training enabling non-native listeners to reach native-level performance.

Note on a gap relative to the research question: this source does NOT address playback speeds above 16x or any hard perceptual ceiling for extreme speed-ups; it caps at roughly 5x compression and explicitly contains no statement about a rate at which compressed speech becomes unintelligible. It is strong on TSM method (WSOLA) and training/adaptation effects, but not on the extreme-speed "speed listening" sub-question.
